Off Calendar Jan-28-2019 Continued To Mar-13-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-22-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Genevieve Guertin, Esq. 415-683-0278 for Plaintiff.
Robert Maltz, Esq. 510-457-3457 for Defendant PAUL CHEN.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares it's views. Plaintiff argues Lackner case. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT PAUL CHEN'S MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Paul Chen's motion to strike those portions of the complaint that seek punitive damages is granted without prejudice to a later motion to amend upon further discovery. See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63 (affirming order striking punitive damages allegations). In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud or malice. Civ. Code 3294(a). "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." Civ. Code 3294(c)(1). Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725. Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-1176. The allegations in the complaint that defendant drove a motor vehicle while illegally texting or otherwise impermissibly using a cellphone or similar electronic device, in and of themselves, do not rise to the level of malice necessary under Civil Code section 3294 to state a claim for punitive damages. See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210 (evidence that snowboarder who struck skier was not "despicable" so as to support punitive damages claim; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her). Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, relied upon by plaintiff, is distinguishable both because it was decided before the statute was amended in 1987 to require a showing of despicable conduct and on its facts. Id. at 1212 & n.14.(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
